title: 12 Predictions for 2024
summary:
Main
By George Lagarias
It's difficult to forecast what 2024 will look like when 2023 isn't over. Simply put, given the stock and bond market volatility, we don't know what the starting point for equity valuations and rates will be in the next thirty days. At the beginning of the year, we said that volatility and geopolitical uncertainty will persist.

children.title: Main
children.summary:
Image reference: assets/2023-12-12_12-predictions-for-2024_img_replay2bbutton_6880508cf732.png
By George Lagarias
It's difficult to forecast what 2024 will look like when 2023 isn't over. Simply put, given the stock and bond market volatility, we don't know what the starting point for equity valuations and rates will be in the next thirty days.

children.title: 1.Geopolitical Tensions and geoeconomic fragmentation will linger.
section_path: 1.Geopolitical Tensions and geoeconomic fragmentation will linger.
children.section_path_text: 1.Geopolitical Tensions and geoeconomic fragmentation will linger.
children.summary: We finish 2023 with two major wars that have geopolitical implications, as well as increasingly tighter global trade conditions. Slow growth and increasing borrowing needs will conserve the competition between key economic actors, incentivising governments to use any economic means at their disposal.

children.title: 2.No sustainable bull market until QT stops.
section_path: 2.No sustainable bull market until QT stops.
children.section_path_text: 2.No sustainable bull market until QT stops.
children.summary: While it is often that we don't notice a bull market until mid-way into the next bear market, we feel that Quantitative Tightening prevents sustainably high equity returns. Quantitative Easing was the foremost driver of equity performance for the fourteen years prior to 2022.

children.title: 3.Something may yet break. Central banks will probably react fast.
section_path: 3.Something may yet break. Central banks will probably react fast.
children.section_path_text: 3.Something may yet break. Central banks will probably react fast.
children.summary: Real rates (ex-inflation), which matter for the economy, continue to rise, as nominal rates stay steady and inflation is falling. All other things being equal, monetary conditions should thus continue to tighten until inflation has dropped sufficiently to force central banks to climb down from the peak, towards the natural rate of interest.

children.title: 4.Rates will begin to come down in mid/late H2 in the US. Europe will follow.
section_path: 4.Rates will begin to come down in mid/late H2 in the US. Europe will follow.
children.section_path_text: 4.Rates will begin to come down in mid/late H2 in the US. Europe will follow.
children.summary: The rate outlook is murky and data /event-driven. As such, the market's confidence level in predicting rates is low.

children.title: 5.Quantitative Tightening will end roughly around the time when rate cuts begin. Equity markets will be able to sustain a rally thereafter.
section_path: 5.Quantitative Tightening will end roughly around the time when rate cuts begin. Equity markets will be able to sustain a rally thereafter.
children.section_path_text: 5.Quantitative Tightening will end roughly around the time when rate cuts begin. Equity markets will be able to sustain a rally thereafter.
children.summary: Markets care less about rates and more about Quantitative Tightening (QT). Central banks, which have reduced their balance sheets aggressively in the past year and a half, have been rather silent about discontinuing the practice.

children.title: 6. Short yields to come down, curve to bull steepen
section_path: 6. Short yields to come down, curve to bull steepen
children.section_path_text: 6. Short yields to come down, curve to bull steepen
children.summary: When central banks hike or cut rates, the short end of the curve moves first and faster. When rate cuts begin, we expect that short rates will come down faster than long rates, allowing the yield curve to Bull Steepen (the whole curve comes down, but faster at the shorter end).

children.title: 7..Economies will slow down in H1 and begin to recover in later H2. No deep recession.
section_path: 7..Economies will slow down in H1 and begin to recover in later H2. No deep recession.
children.section_path_text: 7..Economies will slow down in H1 and begin to recover in later H2. No deep recession.
children.summary: The economic slowdown has been well underway since mid-year 2023. However, we believe that, at least in the US and possibly in the UK we won't see a deep recession.

children.title: 8.Inflation to stabilise, bar geopolitical turbulence
children.summary: Inflation has two components: demand-side and supply-side. Usually, inflation cycles begin with supply-side tensions which then turn into demand (consumer) led pressures.

children.title: 9.Consumption to slow down and unemployment to climb, albeit marginally.
section_path: 9.Consumption to slow down and unemployment to climb, albeit marginally.
children.section_path_text: 9.Consumption to slow down and unemployment to climb, albeit marginally.
children.summary: In this growth and inflation environment, we expect consumption to slow down in the first half of the year, and begin to recover thereafter, as consumers begin to consider the positive economic effects of rate cuts. Unemployment will continue to climb as the economy slows down, however, it has become evident that poor demographics, the persistent skills gap and low labour participation rates will keep employment conditions tight.

children.title: 10. Deficits will persist, and debt will continue to climb.
section_path: 10. Deficits will persist, and debt will continue to climb.
children.section_path_text: 10. Deficits will persist, and debt will continue to climb.
children.summary: Global debt to GDP is 340%, nearly 120% higher than it was two decades ago. Despite high levels of GDP and interest payments exceeding output growth, many countries have opted to maintain high levels of fiscal spending to mitigate the effects of the economic slowdown.

children.title: 11. China will rebound.
children.summary: Although the scenarios vary, we believe that China will see the smoothing of its real estate market crash and a slow return towards growth normality. Economic growth, much of which is still manufacturing-based, is already somewhat rebounding, which can be corroborated by improving industrial data in Asia and other sources.

children.title: 12. The Dollar will remain the world's reserve currency
section_path: 12. The Dollar will remain the world's reserve currency
children.section_path_text: 12. The Dollar will remain the world's reserve currency
children.summary: Despite efforts to dislodge the US Dollar from its natural perch, at the top of the global currencies food chain, we believe that the Greenback will remain the world's choice as a global reserve in the next year, and years to come. Despite China amassing gold reserves and some deals in Asia to trade commodities in local currencies, China and other economies still hold huge amounts of Dollar reserves.
